extracts.extract: We publish our data and methodology
extracts.notes: CBC News practice of publishing data and methodology alongside stories

extracts.extract: But had to learn JS to publish my results… So why not using JS for E-V-E-R-Y-T-H-I-N-G?
extracts.notes: Argument for unifying analysis and presentation in a single language to avoid context-switching between Python/R and JS

extracts.extract: I recommend also installing sqlite-utils - a companion tool to Datasette that makes it easy to create and manipulate the SQLite database files that Datasette uses.
extracts.notes: sqlite-utils as companion CLI for creating/manipulating SQLite databases

extracts.extract: sqlite-utils insert manatees.db locations Manatee_Carcass_Recovery_Locations_in_Florida.csv --csv -d
extracts.notes: Procedural step: importing CSV into SQLite database via sqlite-utils insert command

extracts.extract: sqlite-utils transform manatees.db locations --rename LAT latitude --rename LONG_ longitude --drop created_user --drop last_edited_user --drop X --drop Y --drop STATE --drop OBJECTID --pk FIELDID
extracts.notes: Renaming columns, dropping unnecessary fields, setting primary key via sqlite-utils transform

extracts.extract: datasette install datasette-cluster-map. This installs the datasette-cluster-map Datasette plugin.
extracts.notes: Adding geographic visualization to data via plugin installation

extracts.extract: Datasette was originally designed as a tool for publishing data. The 'datasette publish' command can be used to deploy a full copy of the Datasette application along with bundled data (the Baked Data architectural pattern) to a number of different hosting providers: Vercel - good for data less than 50MB, Google Cloud Run - good for larger datasets, up to about 2GB, Fly.io - good for predictable pricing and read-write volumes
extracts.notes: Baked Data pattern: bundle application + data together for deployment. Size-based hosting recommendations.

extracts.extract: You can upload CSV data to Datasette Cloud, but a more exciting way to import data is to pull it directly from open government data portals that run using Socrata (a common platform for those portals).
extracts.notes: Direct integration with Socrata-based government open data portals

extracts.extract: Datasette Lite is a version of Datasette that runs entirely in your browser, using Python compiled to WebAssembly. Since there's no server-side component to this at all (it's just static HTML and JavaScript served by GitHub) there's very little that can go wrong. This is a very robust free way to share a link to a Datasette interface on top of some data.
extracts.chapter: In your browser with Datasette Lite
extracts.notes: Client-side-only data exploration eliminates server dependencies and hosting costs

extracts.extract: Enrichments: a plugin-based mechanism for 'enriching' data - select a column of addresses and hit 'geocode', select a column of URLs to PDF files and hit 'run OCR', select a column of URLs to YouTube videos and hit 'extract transcripts'.
extracts.notes: Plugin-based column-level enrichment as a design pattern for data augmentation

extracts.extract: Write API: Automatically populate your Datasette instance via a secure API - ideal for running in GitHub Actions and combining with tricks such as git scraping.
extracts.notes: Git scraping: automated periodic data collection via version control

extracts.extract: Plugins are global - if you 'datasette install datasette-cluster-map' it will be installed for anything else using that Datasette installation. BUT if you know how to use Python virtual environments you can run a separate environment for each of your Datasette projects and have different plugins installed in each one.
extracts.notes: Use virtual environments to isolate per-project plugin configurations

extracts.extract: Everything gets backed up regularly. Your original data must be sacrosanct. Make a copy out of email. Then make a copy of that. If you're putting more than 20 minutes into something, you should put it on Github. Make incremental backups of projects, with timestamps in the filename.
extracts.notes: Stucka. Sacrosanct original data + 20-minute Github threshold + timestamped incremental backups.

extracts.extract: Spend more time than you want to thinking about what you're going to do, and you won't regret it. Sketch out each section of your code. Think about your data models. Think about your data models more. Determine how things relate. Review what bridges you need to make things work. Think what you can recycle into this project. Think what this project can get you for future ones.
extracts.notes: Stucka. Emphasis on up-front planning of code structure and data models before writing.

extracts.extract: Track everything for accountability. What you need to complete projects. Data peculiarities and definitions. Decision points. Missing records. Missing decisions. Phone calls.
extracts.notes: Stucka. Track data quirks, decisions, missing records, and communications for accountability.

extracts.extract: Working with APIs: Understand what data it spits out. Read through the API documentation to understand field definitions and possible scenarios, edge cases (so you can anticipate all sorts of possible values that the API spits out). Free tools available to help you understand data structure, e.g. https://jsonformatter.org/json-pretty-print
extracts.notes: Lung. Emphasizes reading API docs for field definitions and anticipating edge cases before building pipelines.

extracts.extract: You can input URL of source data, or link a local file and have Excel load it in tabular format. Get most up-to-date data using Refresh.
extracts.notes: Lung. Using Excel's data connection features to consume live API/CSV data with auto-refresh.

extracts.extract: To manage tools development, I found Trello most useful. TRACK AND PRIORITIZE: My TODOs (must-have features identified for the pipeline and Excel UI), My questions for users / clarifications, Bugs during testing, Add-on requests from users (nice-to-haves). For the end users: Make checklists and guidelines for using the tool to reduce friction + increase adoption.
extracts.notes: Lung. Using kanban boards to manage tool development lifecycle including user-facing documentation.

extracts.extract: TESTING FOR SUCCESS: Always ask: 'What if...?' 'What can possibly break this?' Always think of edge cases (numbers: lower bound, upper bound, zeros; elections: wins/losses/tie/retraction) and where systems might fail. Document for posterity.
extracts.notes: Lung. Systematic edge-case testing with domain-specific examples (elections: ties, retractions).

extracts.extract: Reproducibility. Shared practice. Avoid rabbit holes. Reps on writing clearly about data.
extracts.notes: Hing. Four core goals for personal data journalism workflow.

extracts.extract: Define a list of tasks across projects in a daily note. Further define a task within a project in a data diary entry. Do the task. Quickly inspect new data with VisiData. Write a script/Makefile rule to process data. Analyze data in an executable notebook. File a records request. Follow up with an agency about a data finding. Document what I learned from the task. Note the next step.
extracts.notes: Hing. Complete daily workflow cycle from task definition through execution to documentation.

extracts.extract: Home base for project work is a really long Markdown file. Lives in a project folder with analysis scripts. Editor features help make it navigable despite length.
extracts.notes: Hing. Single long Markdown file as project hub, co-located with analysis scripts.

extracts.extract: Data diary entry: Defines more precisely what I'm going to do. Reminds me what I did in the past in greater detail. Helps others reproduce or validate my work. Will reference or link to scripts, Makefile targets, analysis notebooks, records request trackers, clips.
extracts.notes: Hing. Data diary as reproducibility artifact that cross-references all project components.

extracts.extract: Data source documentation: Tells me where to find source data. Reminds me of things that stood out when I first inspected data. Warns me of ambiguities I need to resolve... or traps to avoid. Gets copied into analysis notebooks.
extracts.notes: Hing. Source documentation that captures provenance, first-inspection observations, and known traps.

extracts.extract: Makefile target: Orchestrates scripts to process data. Provides data that is smaller to analysis notebooks. Simplifies analysis notebooks.
extracts.notes: Hing. Using Makefiles to orchestrate data processing and feed simplified outputs to notebooks.

extracts.extract: Analysis notebook: Starts as an outline of data questions. Evolves into a list of data findings with proof. Draft footnotes link here for fact-checking.
extracts.notes: Hing. Notebooks evolve from questions to findings; draft footnotes link back for verification.

extracts.extract: Do repeat yourself. Use the tool for the job. Link between things when possible. Reference items that can't be linked with easily searchable identifiers such as citations. Copy and paste if there's not an easier alternative. It's ok for some information to become outdated, but pay attention to which places need to be high fidelity (e.g. analysis notebooks). Having to re-write the same thing helps hone the language about data process and findings.
extracts.notes: Hing. Contrarian 'do repeat yourself' principle: rewriting hones language; distinguish high-fidelity from ephemeral docs.

extracts.extract: Documenting my process parallels writing about data for an audience. How did I get here? Why does it matter? What do I know? What do I want to know? What is the first, next step? I find that when I describe my data reporting process for co-reporters and editors, and for my future self, some of the things I want to convey to them, are the same things I want to convey to readers when I write about findings and methodology. Ultimately, it's all about trust in the rigor of reporting.
extracts.notes: Hing. Process documentation and public methodology writing are fundamentally the same act -- both build trust.

extracts.extract: When I start a project: Running questions. One page - ReadMe, roadmap. Should answer my high-level questions about the topic. Links, links everywhere. Clips. Timeline (if helpful). Reminders / memos. Actionable items.
extracts.notes: Reyes. Single-page project roadmap with running questions, links, timeline, and actionable items.

extracts.extract: Remove yourself. Change of scenery. Remove distractions. Build a zone. Paper notes.
extracts.notes: Stucka. Physical distance from computers and analog note-taking to support deeper thinking.

extracts.extract: Daily log: Helps me plan my day. Reminds me what I did last week/last month/3 months ago. Can reflect multiple projects and things that aren't reporting.
extracts.notes: Hing. Daily log as cross-project planning and memory tool.

extracts.extract: When I start work, I update a 'log' and generate TODOs as needed. If existing project, link up research, information received.
extracts.notes: Reyes. Daily startup: update log, generate TODOs, link incoming research to projects.

extracts.extract: OpenRefine can help you clean big datasets even if you don't know how to code. Learn how to open a CSV file in OpenRefine. Learn how to use OpenRefine to pivot data and make it easier to work with. Learn faceting, clustering. Export cleaned data.
extracts.notes: OpenRefine as low-code data cleaning tool with pivot, facet, and cluster capabilities.

extracts.extract: This will 'pivot' the data from individual columns for each year to putting all of the years into one column and creating a bunch more rows, essentially making it longer and thinner. Select Transpose and then Transpose Cells Across Columns into Rows. Make sure the box marked Fill down in other columns is checked.
extracts.notes: Converting wide-format data to long-format using OpenRefine's Transpose function.

extracts.extract: While I generally use simple R commands to pivot my data, I still will often run data through OpenRefine before I open it in RStudio because it is very smart at recognizing patterns in cells that might take me longer to address in an R script.
extracts.notes: Using OpenRefine for pattern-based cleaning even when R is available, because its clustering algorithms are faster for messy text.

extracts.extract: Clustering is my favorite function within OpenRefine, because it can allow you to quickly clean up a big dataset by identifying similar text values without a bunch of fancy fuzzy matching or other complex processes. OpenRefine uses two overarching clustering categories. The first, key collision, creates alternate versions of strings that contain only the most meaningful parts (the key part), then buckets the strings together based on whether those keys match (the collision part). The second, nearest neighbor, compares all the different strings to each other and bins them when they are similar enough. But nearest neighbor methods take forever for big datasets because each pair needs to be compared to every other pair.
extracts.notes: Two clustering strategies: key collision (efficient, fewer errors) vs nearest neighbor (thorough but slow). Key collision preferred for large datasets.

extracts.extract: The first, fingerprint, is the default key collision method, because it tends to make the fewest mistakes (fingerprint groups different strings together by removing whitespace, changing all characters to lowercase, removing punctuation and several other intuitive steps.) There's also metaphone3, a method that transforms strings into how they are typically pronounced. This is useful for errors that involve misspellings.
extracts.notes: Fingerprint (safest default) then metaphone (pronunciation-based) as sequential cleaning strategy.

extracts.extract: Select Edit cells -> Common transforms -> Trim leading and trailing whitespace. This removes any spaces that are in the cells either before or after the city name. Now let's do one other easy transform, Edit cells -> Common transforms -> To titlecase.
extracts.notes: Simple transforms (trim whitespace, titlecase) should be applied before clustering for quick wins.

extracts.extract: This Undo/Redo pane, with all of our steps listed in a linear fashion, is also helpful to use in the future, should we encounter similar data and want to clean it quickly. Click the Extract button and the window Extract Operation History should pop up. Check the tasks that you'd want to repeat on similar data. Open a TextEdit window and paste the code you generated into a new window and save it with a .json extension. Now if you want to apply the same commands to a similar data file, you can\! Just hit Apply, paste the .json script you saved into the window, and watch the replicable magic happen.
extracts.notes: Export cleaning steps as JSON for reuse on similar datasets. Key reproducibility feature of OpenRefine.

extracts.extract: Some of the values have parentheses to indicate they are negative amounts, and some have commas while others do not. These characters and the dollar symbols are making the column get recognized as a text column rather than as numeric. You can click on Edit Cells and then click Replace. Type in an opening parenthesis into the Find box and then in the Replace box, type in a dash. Then get rid of the other three annoying characters by typing them into the Find box and leaving the Replace box blank. Once you do this, select Edit Cells, Common Transforms, To Number.
extracts.notes: Step-by-step: strip parentheses/commas/dollar signs then convert to numeric. Common pattern for financial data.

extracts.extract: A regular expression is a common, compact way to represent some sort of pattern in text. You can use one to: Help you search within a document. Reformat or otherwise clean up an inconsistent or 'dirty' data set. Extract structured data from plain text. Many common programming languages, text editors and search tools support regular expressions, so you can use them in whatever environment you find most comfortable.
extracts.notes: Myers/AP. Three use cases for regex: search, clean, extract. Portable across languages and editors.

extracts.extract: Another is to save those groups so you can refer to them later, such as in the replacement step of a find-and-replace operation. These often are referenced by their position; for example, in (\d\d\d)-(\d\d\d)-(\d\d\d\d), you might use \1 to refer to the first three digits, \2 to refer to the second three digits and \3 to refer to the last four digits.
extracts.notes: Myers/AP. Using regex capture groups for structured reformatting of text data.

extracts.extract: Different programming languages, text editors and other tools handle regular expressions in their own ways. Some environments have different rules about when certain characters do and don't need to be escaped. Some languages such as R require backslashes themselves to be escaped, so you might have to type twice as many backslashes as you otherwise would think.
extracts.notes: Myers/AP. Regex syntax varies by environment; R double-escaping is a common gotcha.

extracts.extract: Average/Mean - Most common (Total/# of items). Median - Middle value. Better to show typical value. Mode: Value that comes up the most.
extracts.notes: Wallack/WBUR. Median recommended over mean for showing typical values.

extracts.extract: Standard Deviation: How far numbers differ from the median. In a normal curve, 68% of values are within 1 standard deviation. 95% are within 2. And 99.7% are within three. So a difference of 2 standard deviations is usually significant. Outliers are generally beyond three standard deviations. Standard Error: How much numbers of a sample are likely to differ from the true value in the overall population. Bigger samples have lower standard errors.
extracts.notes: Wallack/WBUR. Rule of thumb: 2 SD = significant, 3 SD = outlier. Standard error decreases with sample size.

extracts.extract: Per capita rates are 'per person' or per unit. Number / Population. But you can convert to other rates like Per 1,000 or Per 100,000. Best to use a rate that allows you to express numbers more clearly. Like 15 homicides per 100,000 people. Instead of .00015 gun deaths per person or per capita. Experiment with the rate that works best.
extracts.notes: Wallack/WBUR. Choose rate denominator for readability; avoid tiny decimals.

extracts.extract: Correlation is between 0 (weak) and 1 (strong). Negative correlations mean when one value goes up, the other goes down.
extracts.notes: Wallack/WBUR. Basic correlation interpretation for journalists.

extracts.extract: Regression only works on numerical values, like salaries and height. If you want to convert a categorical variable to numerical, you need to turn it into a numerical value (often called a dummy variable). So for instance, if you have a gender column with categories like male, female and nonbinary, you might create a new column called 'female' that has 1 for female and 0 for not female.
extracts.notes: Wallack/WBUR. Creating dummy variables for categorical data to enable regression analysis.

extracts.extract: To compare coefficients to see which one is most important, standardize values. The formula is =standardize(x,average,stdev)
extracts.notes: Wallack/WBUR. Standardize regression coefficients to compare relative importance of predictors.

extracts.extract: Some of this stuff is complicated, particularly when it comes to regression. Before you publish a story, it often makes sense to contact a statistician or researcher who will look over your analysis and make sure you are not missing anything.
extracts.notes: Wallack/WBUR. Seek external statistical review before publishing regression-based findings.

extracts.extract: Real-time results dashboard in a local spreadsheet\! Excel 'magic': Macros (for auto refresh, sound alerts), Conditional highlights, Formulas.
extracts.notes: Lung. Using Excel macros and conditional formatting for real-time election results monitoring.

extracts.extract: Zotero notes: Ties source document and takeaway. Linked from data diary, analysis notebook and, eventually draft.
extracts.notes: Hing. Zotero as connective tissue linking source documents to data diary, notebooks, and final draft.

extracts.extract: For advanced visualizations I like to use Observable Notebooks. Since it's easy to get JSON or CSV data out of Datasette by URL you can build notebooks that use data from it.
extracts.notes: Willison. Datasette's URL-based data export feeds directly into Observable for visualization.

extracts.extract: Skewness: Measures the shape of your curve. Positive skewness indicates a distribution with an asymmetric tail extending toward more positive values. Kurtosis: A measure of how often outliers occur (at one of the tails). Bigger numbers tend to have long thin tails.
extracts.notes: Wallack/WBUR. Distribution shape measures: skewness for asymmetry, kurtosis for tail heaviness.
